candidates.snippet:
permissions:
  contents: read
  id-token: write  # required for npm OIDC trusted publishing + provenance

jobs:

permissions:
      contents: read
      id-token: write  # required for npm OIDC trusted publishing + provenance
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@11bd71901bbe5b1630ceea73d27597364c9af683 # v4.2.2

findings.title: Unpinned GitHub Actions in release workflow with id-token: write and npm publish
findings.description: The release workflow grants `id-token: write` (L42) and runs `npm publish --access public` (L106) using npm OIDC Trusted Publishing. It consumes `actions/checkout@v4` (L49), `pnpm/action-setup@v4` (L76), and `actions/setup-node@v4` (L79) by mutable major-version tag. If any of these tags is hijacked (compromised maintainer, force-push to v4), the malicious code runs in the same job that has `id-token: write` and is recognized by npm's Trusted Publisher as the legitimate `release.yml` workflow — so the action can request its own OIDC token, exchange it with npm, and publish a malicious `deepsec` package version (or simply tamper with files between checkout and `npm publish`). The repo's `prepublishOnly`/`prepack` hooks run inside the same context and offer no defense. Combined with `id-token: write`, an unpinned action is effectively a supply-chain backdoor for everyone who installs deepsec from npm.
findings.recommendation: Pin every `uses:` in this workflow to a full 40-char commit SHA with a trailing version comment. Strongly consider also (1) moving the publish step into a dedicated minimal-permissions job that runs *after* a separate validation job, (2) using a GitHub Actions Environment with required reviewers gating the publish step, and (3) limiting `id-token: write` to the publish job only via job-level `permissions:` rather than workflow-level. Re-enable `--provenance` once the repo is public so consumers can verify build provenance.

revalidation.reasoning: The current release workflow no longer matches the vulnerable state described in the finding. Workflow-level permissions are only `contents: read`, and `id-token: write` is scoped to the `publish` job rather than granted globally. More importantly, every `uses:` action in the publish job is pinned to a full 40-character commit SHA: `actions/checkout` at line 51, `pnpm/action-setup` at line 78, and `actions/setup-node` at line 81. The npm publish step still runs with OIDC Trusted Publishing at lines 106-108, so the original risk model would be valid if these actions were mutable major tags. In the current code, however, a hijacked `@v4` tag would not affect this workflow because the workflow does not reference those tags. I could not inspect git history because `/vercel/sandbox/target` is not a git repository in this environment, but the current source shows the recommended mitigation has been applied.
